topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash. Elias stared at the printout on the desk, the stark blue text blurring slightly at the edges. He’d been running the same diagnostic sequence for three days, the fluorescent lights of his tiny apartment buzzing with an irritating constancy. Finally, the results materialized: a match. A profound, undeniable genetic link to a family he’d never known. A heavy slump settled into his shoulders, pulling at his spine. He rubbed his eyes with the heels of his hands, a slow, deliberate motion that seemed to require immense effort. The air in the room felt thick, pressing down on his chest. He just wanted the noise to stop, the relentless hum of existence to fade into silence.

[story 2]

Maya leaned her head against the cool windowpane, the city lights outside a smear of indifferent color. Her fingers traced the raised lines on the DNA report, the paper feeling flimsy beneath her touch. A deep weariness seeped into her bones, a pervasive dullness that made lifting her arm feel like moving through water. She reread the matching percentages, the sheer improbability of the connection making her breath catch in her throat. A low sigh escaped her lips, a sound stripped of any real energy. She pushed the chair back, the scrape against the wooden floor sounding disproportionately loud in the quiet space. Everything felt weighted, a slow, exhausting drag.

[story 3]

He traced the unfamiliar name on the screen, the letters seeming distant and foreign. David kept blinking, his eyelids feeling weighted with lead. The effort of processing the implications—the sudden, vast expansion of his personal history—was draining him completely. He picked up a glass of water, his hand shaking just slightly as he brought it to his lips. The taste was flat, uninteresting. He let his gaze drift toward the ceiling, watching a dust mote dance in a stray beam of light. There was a profound stillness about him now, a complete cessation of forward momentum, as if his very system had simply decided to power down.
